column1: Plato (in Timaeus) associated the five regular solids with the classical elements: Cube (Earth), Icosahedron (Water), Tetrahedron (Fire), Octahedron (Air), and Dodecahedron (Aether/Universe)127.
These associations influenced later Western esoteric and alchemical traditions.
Aristotle added Aether as the "fifth element" to the four classical elements.
Hermeticism (Corpus Hermeticum) and Western alchemy adopted the same elemental scheme, sometimes adding Spirit as a unifying principle.
The ten Sefirot are not elements but are energy centers or emanations from the divine, sometimes compared to chakras in modern syncretic systems.
Focuses on the metaphysical hierarchy: The One, Nous (Intellect), and Soul, sometimes mapped to spiritual centers or elements in later esoteric traditions.
Citations: Use direct references for each association, especially where modern syncretism (chakras + Platonic solids) occurs246.
Distinction: Clearly distinguish between original cultural contexts (e.g., Indian chakras, Platonic elements) and modern Western reinterpretations.
Completeness: Include all major Western systems that use elements or energy centers: Platonic, Aristotelian, Hermetic, Kabbalistic, Neoplatonic, Theosophical, and New Age.
Color Associations: Note that rainbow color associations with chakras are a 20th-century Western addition, not found in ancient sources6.
Modern Syncretism: Clearly indicate which correspondences are traditional and which are modern inventions or reinterpretations6.
Platonic solids and their elemental associations:123458
Western chakra systems and their development:6

This expanded, cited, and structured approach provides a comprehensive, accurate, and cross-cultural overview of chakra-like and elemental systems, including both Eastern and Western traditions and their modern syncretic developments.
